risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "In order to randomly allocate either of the interventions to the participants of the study, block permutation design was undertaken with ten blocks including four patients in each block. The file number of all the participants eligible to take part in the study was provided for the biostatistician. Assigning each number to either of the interventions was carried out by the biostatistician." Comment: This describes a block permutation randomization process with allocation handled by a biostatistician, which is sufficient to indicate a proper random sequence was used. Although the exact random number generation method is not stated, the use of block permutation design and random allocation supports Low risk.

Quote: "The allocation sequence was concealed from the orthodontist until after the placement of the retainer." Comment: This is explicit evidence that the recruiter/enrolling clinician could not foresee the next assignment before enrollment/placement, which satisfies allocation concealment. Although the paper also notes that the biostatistician assigned numbers to interventions, the key point for this domain is the explicit concealment of the allocation sequence from the orthodontist.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a single-blind (to the assessor of the results), parallel-group" and "Once the retainers were placed, the participants were instructed by the same dentist who placed the retainers to undertake meticulous oral hygiene..." Comment: Only the outcome assessor was blinded; participants and the dentist delivering the retainers/intervention were not reported as blinded. Because the main outcomes were subjective oral-health indices (plaque, gingival index, calculus, bleeding on probing) that could plausibly be influenced by participant or personnel knowledge of allocation and care advice, performance bias is at high risk.

Quote: "This was a single-blind (to the assessor of the results)" and "The assessment and scoring of the indices were carried out by a calibrated dentist." Also, "The radiologists were blinded to the study and therefore not aware whether the radiographs were taken at the beginning of the trial or after 6 months." Comment: The clinical periodontal outcomes (plaque index, calculus index, gingival index, bleeding on probing) were assessed by a blinded/calibrated dentist, so outcome assessment was blinded. The radiographic outcome was also assessed by blinded radiologists. These outcomes are partly subjective/judgement-dependent, so assessor blinding is important and was reported, indicating low risk of detection bias.

Quote: "Forty patients were randomly selected for this clinical trial and were divided into two groups. Ten patients were withdrawn from the study either because they missed their final appointment (n = 3 in the spiral wire group and n = 2 in the fiber-reinforced group) or because a breakage was diagnosed along the retainer... Therefore, they had to be withdrawn from further evaluation... The total sample size therefore was 30 (15 in each group)" Comment: Attrition was reported and appears reasonably balanced between groups (5 vs 5 withdrawals overall when the broken-retainer cases are included), with comparable reasons across arms. Although 10/40 participants were withdrawn, the paper explains the reasons and they are not obviously differential or outcome-related in a way that would bias the between-group comparison; the final analyzed groups were equal in size.
